content: Aspirin is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that inhibits cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2), reducing prostaglandin synthesis and thereby decreasing inflammation, pain, and fever.

The acute inflammatory response is characterized by five cardinal signs: redness (rubor), heat (calor), swelling (tumor), pain (dolor), and loss of function (functio laesa). Mediators include histamine, bradykinin, and prostaglandins.
Edema results from increased vascular permeability allowing plasma proteins and fluid to leak into the interstitial space. It is a hallmark of acute inflammation.

Paracetamol (acetaminophen) lowers fever and relieves mild-to-moderate pain by acting centrally on the hypothalamic heat-regulating center. Unlike NSAIDs, it has weak peripheral anti-inflammatory activity.
Cytokines such as TNF-alpha, IL-1, and IL-6 are central drivers of the systemic inflammatory response and the acute-phase reaction, including fever and leukocytosis.
Corticosteroids suppress inflammation by inhibiting phospholipase A2, decreasing arachidonic acid release, and downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokine transcription.
Erythema is the redness of skin or mucous membranes caused by hyperemia of superficial capillaries; it is a common sign of cutaneous inflammation.
Histamine, released from mast cells during allergic and inflammatory reactions, increases vascular permeability and causes vasodilation in postcapillary venules.
Myocardial infarction occurs when coronary blood flow is interrupted, most commonly by atherosclerotic plaque rupture and thrombus formation, leading to ischemic necrosis of myocardium.
Chronic inflammation is dominated by macrophages, lymphocytes, and plasma cells. It is associated with tissue destruction and concomitant repair by fibrosis and angiogenesis.
Ibuprofen, a propionic acid derivative NSAID, reversibly inhibits both COX-1 and COX-2 and is widely used for inflammation, pain, and fever in adults and children.
The complement cascade contributes to inflammation through anaphylatoxins (C3a, C5a), which cause mast-cell degranulation, smooth-muscle contraction, and leukocyte chemotaxis.
Neutrophils are the first leukocytes recruited to sites of acute bacterial infection; they phagocytose pathogens and release reactive oxygen species and proteases.
Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease characterized by bronchial hyper-responsiveness, eosinophilic infiltration, and reversible airflow obstruction.
Resolution of inflammation requires apoptosis of neutrophils, efferocytosis by macrophages, and the action of specialized pro-resolving mediators such as resolvins and protectins.
